full_response:
Let 0<ε<1 be given.  
Throughout we call two routers compatible if the distance between their centres is ≥2,
i.e. their unit–radius coverage disks do not overlap.

================================================================
Algorithm
================================================================
1.   k ← ⌈4/ε⌉                                ▹ a constant that depends only on ε  
2.   Pick two real numbers α,β independently and uniformly at random
     from the half-open interval [0 , k).  
     Draw the two infinite families of straight lines  
           x = α + ik  and  y = β + jk       (i ,j ∈ ℤ)
     The lines partition the plane into congruent k×k squares, which
     we call grid cells.

3.   A router is declared BAD if its centre lies at distance ≤1
     from any grid line, and GOOD otherwise.

4.   For every grid cell C
        let R(C) ← set of GOOD routers whose centres lie in C.
        Because the diameter of C is k < ∞ and the disks have
        radius 1, at most           p = ⌈(k+2)^2 /π⌉ = Θ(k^2) = Θ(1/ε^2)
        pairwise-compatible routers can be placed in C.
        Enumerate every subset of R(C) of size ≤ p               ▹ p=O(1/ε²) is a constant
        and keep the largest compatible subset; call it S(C).

5.   Output S = ⋃C S(C).

================================================================
Correctness
================================================================
• Routers coming from two different cells are ≥ k−2 ≥2 units apart
  (because their centres are separated by at least k in one
  coordinate and we removed every router that is ≤1 away from
  the separating grid line).  Hence no two routers returned by
  the algorithm interfere; S is a feasible set.

================================================================
Approximation ratio
================================================================
Let OPT be an optimal feasible set and let r∈OPT be an arbitrary
router.  Consider the vertical grid lines.

Distance(centre(r), closest vertical grid line) is uniformly
distributed in [0 , k).  Router r is declared BAD because of the
vertical lines only if this distance is ≤1; that happens with
probability 2/k.  The same argument works for the horizontal
lines and contributes another 2/k.  By the union bound

        Pr[r is BAD] ≤ 4/k ≤ ε      (since k=⌈4/ε⌉).

Therefore the expected number of BAD routers inside OPT is at
most ε·|OPT|, and the expected number of GOOD routers inside OPT
is at least (1−ε)|OPT|.

The algorithm keeps **every** GOOD router that OPT could possibly
keep inside each cell, because it computes S(C) optimally.
Hence

        E[|S|] ≥ (1−ε)|OPT|.

Thus the algorithm is a (1−ε)-approximation in expectation.

================================================================
Running time
================================================================
• The plane contains at most n cells that contain at least one
  input router.  
• In every cell C we enumerate ≤ |R(C)|^p subsets, where
      p = Θ(1/ε²) is a constant and |R(C)| ≤ n.
  Hence the time per cell is |R(C)|^{Θ(1/ε²)}, and the total
  running time is

        Σ_C |R(C)|^{Θ(1/ε²)} ≤ n^{Θ(1/ε²)} ,

  which is polynomial in n for every fixed ε>0.  The algorithm
  uses only O(n) space.

================================================================
Conclusion
================================================================
For any fixed accuracy parameter 0<ε<1 we have given a randomized
algorithm that

  • runs in n^{Θ(1/ε²)} time (polynomial),
  • outputs a feasible non-interfering set of routers, and
  • has expected size at least (1−ε)·|OPT|.

Hence the problem admits a randomized polynomial-time
(1−ε)-approximation scheme.

\boxed{\text{Random shifting + exact optimisation in small squares
 gives an expected }(1-\varepsilon)\text{-approximation in
 polynomial time.}}

Confidence: 71%
